title: Trust and Outrage
thesis: In India, engagement-based ranking and weak accountability mechanisms systematically amplify outrage and divisive content, eroding the social trust that public problem-solving requires.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: On major social platforms, engagement-based ranking systematically amplifies emotionally charged and divisive content because anger and moral outrage generate more likes, shares, and comments than neutral or constructive material.

evidence.snippet: Milli et al. found that Twitter's engagement-based ranking algorithm amplifies emotionally charged, out-group hostile content compared with a chronological timeline.
Yale researchers found that social-media rewards (likes and shares) reinforce moral outrage expression over time.

counterevidence.summary: Platform-specific results vary by user base, content type, and ranking objective; not every platform uses pure engagement ranking, and some experiments show that alternative feeds can preserve user satisfaction.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

claims.claim: Users who receive more likes and shares for expressing moral outrage tend to express more outrage over time, especially in politically moderate networks.

evidence.snippet: A Science Advances study of 12.7 million tweets found that users who received more social rewards for outrage expressed more outrage later; the effect was strongest in politically moderate networks.

counterevidence.summary: The study is based on Twitter data and may not generalize to other platforms or to non-English Indian users; some users may express outrage regardless of rewards.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

claims.claim: On Twitter/X, engagement-based feeds contained a higher share of angry political tweets than chronological feeds in a large-scale audit.

evidence.snippet: In the Milli et al. audit, 62% of political tweets in the engagement-based timeline expressed anger, compared with 52% in the chronological timeline.

counterevidence.summary: The finding is platform- and time-specific; chronological feeds may have other downsides such as increased exposure to harassment or spam.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

claims.claim: Indian influencers on Twitter received higher retweet rates for polarizing-event tweets than for their other tweets in a 2022 Microsoft Research Bengaluru study.

evidence.snippet: Dash et al. found that 84% of influencers had a higher median retweet rate for tweets related to polarizing events compared with their other tweets.

counterevidence.summary: The study focuses on influencers and politicians on Twitter during crises; results may not extend to ordinary users or to platforms like Meta, YouTube, or WhatsApp.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

claims.claim: The World Economic Forum's Global Risks Report 2024 ranked India highest among surveyed countries for misinformation and disinformation risk over the next two years.

evidence.snippet: The WEF Global Risks Report 2024 identifies misinformation and disinformation as the top risk in India among surveyed countries over the two-year horizon.

counterevidence.summary: The WEF ranking is based on expert perception, not a direct measurement of misinformation exposure or harm; perceptions may reflect media salience rather than objective prevalence.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

claims.claim: India Hate Lab documented a 74.4% increase in hate-speech events targeting religious minorities in 2024, with digital platforms playing a significant amplification role.

evidence.snippet: India Hate Lab documented 1,165 hate-speech events targeting religious minorities in 2024, up 74.4% from 668 events in 2023, and noted platform amplification.

counterevidence.summary: India Hate Lab is an advocacy-oriented watchdog; its event definition and sampling methodology may be challenged by other observers, and offline political organization also drives the trend.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

claims.claim: In a sampled set of viral WhatsApp groups in India, only one in 158 instances of misinformation was actively corrected inside the group chat.

evidence.snippet: Garimella et al. identified 158 instances of misinformation in India and found only one instance of active correction or rebuttal within the group.

counterevidence.summary: The sample was limited to consenting private WhatsApp groups and may not represent all Indian WhatsApp use; corrections may also occur through private replies or outside the studied groups.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

claims.claim: Central and South Asia accounted for only 5% of cases selected by the Meta Oversight Board in 2023, despite India being Meta's largest user market.

evidence.snippet: The Meta Oversight Board 2023 annual report states that Central and South Asia represented 5% of selected cases.

counterevidence.summary: The Oversight Board selects a tiny fraction of appeals for review, and case-share is an imperfect proxy for safety investment, staffing, or user harm in the region.

verification.note: Backlog triage (meta#61, site#119): not yet verified. Stamped draft so the backlog is tracked per claim; verify with npm run verify:claim.

sources.title: Brady et al., How social learning amplifies moral outrage expression in online social networks, Science Advances (2021)
Milli et al., Engagement, user satisfaction, and the amplification of divisive content on social media, PNAS Nexus (2025)
Dash et al., Divided We Rule: Influencer Polarization on Twitter during Political Crises in India, ICWSM (2022)
World Economic Forum, Global Risks Report 2024
India Hate Lab, Hate Speech Events in India 2024 Report
Garimella et al., Global Patterns of Viral Content on WhatsApp, ICWSM 2025 (arXiv:2407.08172)

agentInstructions: Use claim IDs as the retrieval unit and distinguish core claims about platform mechanisms from landscape claims about India-specific risk data.
Preserve the evidence-cautious tone; do not present correlation as causation or global platform studies as India-specific findings.
When citing sources, use the source IDs and URLs from the artifact; do not generalize beyond the public evidence.
Flag any source that is advocacy-oriented, industry-funded, or based on limited samples when answering questions about confidence.
Route questions about platform-design alternatives to the Designing for Substance article and series guide.

accepted.rationale: Accepted as a quality backlog item per docs/diagnostics-triage-2026-07-15.md. The claim rests on sources that are sufficient for the current published version; additional independent sources will be added in a future research wave.
